summary: I am the chief of maintenance at Shenandoah National Park, and I also serve as the Operations Section Chief for the National Park Service Incident Management Team. Shenandoah is a large park east of the Mississippi and has a competent Type-3 Incident Management Team made up of park employees. During the 9/11 attacks, I was based at Shenandoah Dispatch, which is the national dispatch center, and we coordinated many of the initial responses from there.

I have worked extensively with the National Park Service, especially in developing and participating in their national incident management teams since about 1990. The Park Service adopted a fire model command structure for incident management, which I have been part of for over a decade. During events like Pearl Harbor commemorations and the 9/11 attacks, the National Park Service coordinated resources across many parks, including law enforcement and emergency personnel.

I began my involvement with incident management about 15 years ago while working special events at Independence National Historical Park. This experience led me into the incident command structure and eventually into the National Park Service's national incident management teams. Independence National Historical Park was one of the key sites we had to consider during national incidents like 9/11.

I have been a member of the National Park Service Incident Management Team since the early 1990s and have participated in every incident since the Pearl Harbor commemorative event in 1991. That event was one of the first major incidents where this incident management structure was tested. It helped establish the framework we use today for managing large-scale events.

I was in the Outer Banks of North Carolina when the 9/11 attacks happened, and I watched the second plane hit the World Trade Center while getting a haircut. I immediately contacted Shenandoah Dispatch and the incident commander to prepare for deployment. We were on the ground by 10 p.m. that Tuesday night, coordinating a national response that involved prioritizing resources to protect sites like the Statue of Liberty, Independence Hall, and Old Ironsides. The incident required 24-hour operational periods and constant revision of plans to manage the evolving situation.

entity: Office of the Secretary of the Interior
summary: During the 9/11 incident, our National Park Service Incident Management Team worked closely with the Office of the Secretary of the Interior. Someone from the Secretary's office was directly involved in setting objectives and responsibilities for our team. We were tasked with coordinating resources nationally and reporting back to the Secretary's office on the status and allocation of Park Service resources.
cues: Office of the Secretary of the Interior

summary: Philadelphia was one of the key locations involved in the national response to 9/11. The Regional Director there called on the Type-2 Incident Management Team to coordinate efforts. Our national team worked to prioritize resources across Philadelphia, New York City, Boston, and other sites to ensure effective protection and response.

New York City was a central focus during the 9/11 attacks, especially with the World Trade Center being the target. The Northeast Region's Type-2 Incident Management Team was dispatched to Philadelphia but managed resources related to New York City. I personally witnessed the attacks while in North Carolina and knew our teams would be heavily involved in the response there.

Boston was one of the cities where the National Park Service had to consider resource allocation during the 9/11 incident. Along with Philadelphia and New York City, Boston was part of the regional coordination efforts to protect key sites and manage emergency responses. Our national team ensured that resources were prioritized effectively across these locations.

Lake Mead was significant during the 9/11 response because of concerns about dam security. As part of the national incident management effort, we had to prioritize resources to protect critical infrastructure like dams at Lake Mead. This required balancing resources between sites like Lake Mead, the Statue of Liberty, and Independence Hall to ensure all were adequately protected.

The Statue of Liberty was one of the iconic sites we had to protect during the 9/11 response. Our national incident management team had to carefully prioritize resources to ensure its security alongside other important sites like Independence Hall and Old Ironsides. This was part of a broader effort to look at the incident from a national perspective and allocate limited resources effectively.

Old Ironsides was among the historic sites we were tasked with protecting during the 9/11 incident. Along with the Statue of Liberty and Independence Hall, it was a priority for resource allocation by the National Park Service Incident Management Team. Protecting such landmarks was part of our holistic national approach to the incident.

While my primary focus has been with the National Park Service, I am aware that other Interior agencies and departments like the United States Department of Agriculture have their own resources. However, during incidents like 9/11, our team primarily managed Park Service resources, which include law enforcement, emergency medical, and maintenance personnel.

The United States Forest Service is another agency with incident management capabilities similar to the National Park Service. While I have not directly managed their resources, I know that the Park Service incident management teams coordinate primarily with their own resources during national incidents. The Forest Service's fire model influenced the structure we use in incident command.

During the 9/11 attacks, the Secretary of the Interior, assistant secretaries, and the National Park Service director were moved to a bunker in West Virginia for safety. This was part of the rapid response and protective measures taken early in the incident. Our incident management team coordinated closely with leadership during this time.

I was in the Outer Banks of North Carolina visiting my parents when the 9/11 attacks occurred. It was there, in a local barber shop, that I watched the second plane hit the World Trade Center. From that moment, I knew I would be called to duty and immediately began coordinating with Shenandoah Dispatch and the incident commander.

I was in North Carolina, specifically the Outer Banks, attending to family matters when the 9/11 attacks happened. Despite being away from work, I was prepared with my go-bag and government emergency telephone system card, allowing me to quickly respond when the National Park Service Incident Management Team was activated. North Carolina was the place where I first learned of the attacks and began mobilizing.
